steps.title: Recall what standard deviation measures
steps.content: Standard deviation measures how spread out values are from the mean. A distribution where most values cluster tightly near the mean has a small standard deviation; one where values are spread far from the mean has a large standard deviation.

steps.title: Examine group A (mean = 2.1)
steps.content: Group A’s histogram shows most of its frequency pushed toward the outer weights (0 and 4), which are about 2 grams away from the mean of 2.1. Few objects sit near 2.1. The data is spread across the full range of the histogram.

steps.title: Examine group B (mean = 11.1)
steps.content: Group B’s histogram shows the tallest bar is at weight 11, extremely close to the mean of 11.1. Most objects cluster tightly around 11.1, with very few in the tails at 9 and 13.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A: The standard deviation of weight for the objects in group A is less than the standard deviation of weight for the objects in group B. — Group A’s values are more spread from its mean than group B’s values are from theirs, so A’s SD is larger, not smaller. Eliminated.
  • Choice B: The standard deviation of weight for the objects in group A is equal to the standard deviation of weight for the objects in group B. — The spreads are clearly different; A is more dispersed. Eliminated.
  • Choice C: The standard deviation of weight for the objects in group A is greater than the standard deviation of weight for the objects in group B. — Group A has more frequency far from its mean, group B is tightly clustered near its mean, so group A has a greater standard deviation. That matches Choice C — The standard deviation of weight for the objects in group A is greater than the standard deviation of weight for the objects in group B..
  • Choice D: There is not enough information to compare the standard deviations of weight for the objects in these two groups. — The histograms give all the data needed to compare spreads. Eliminated.
